section.title: Let the Peoples Praise You
section.sectionRationale: A brief harvest prayer built on the Aaronic blessing, this psalm asks God to bless Israel not as an end in itself but so that His way and salvation would be known among all nations, joining a specific, tangible harvest to the missionary hope that every people on earth would come to praise the true God.
section.thesis: God's blessing on His own people is never meant to stop with them, for His grace toward Israel exists so that His way may be known on earth and His salvation among all nations, a missionary purpose fulfilled in the gospel going out to every people through Christ, in whom the whole earth is called to praise God and fear Him.
section.frameNote: Three movements: a prayer, echoing the Aaronic blessing, that God's grace on His people would make His way known among the nations, verses 1-2; a refrain calling all peoples to praise God as He judges justly and guides the nations, verses 3-5; and the closing report that the earth has yielded its harvest, with the hope that all the earth will fear Him, verses 6-7.

units.label: That Your ways may be known on earth
units.note: the Aaronic blessing prayed for a missionary purpose

verses.text: May God be gracious to us and bless us, and cause His face to {{panim|shine}} upon us, Selah
that Your ways may be known on earth, Your salvation among all nations.

units.label: Let the peoples praise You, O God
units.note: the refrain calling all nations to praise the just Judge

verses.text: Let the peoples praise You, O God; let all the peoples praise You.
Let the nations be glad and sing for joy, for You judge the peoples justly and lead the nations of the earth. Selah
Let the peoples praise You, O God; let all the peoples praise You.

units.label: The earth has yielded its harvest
units.note: the closing hope that all the earth will fear God

verses.text: The earth has yielded its harvest; God, our God, blesses us.
God blesses us, that all the ends of the earth shall {{yare|fear}} Him.

panim.gloss: Face, presence. To have God's face shine is to enjoy His favorable, personal presence, the language of the Aaronic blessing, Numbers 6:24-26, here repurposed not as a private comfort but as the very means by which the nations will come to know God's way.

yare.gloss: To fear, to stand in reverent awe. The psalm's closing hope is that this reverence for God would extend to 'all the ends of the earth,' turning God's specific blessing on Israel into a global, missionary purpose.

basic.context: This short psalm echoes the priestly blessing of Numbers 6, but turns it outward, asking God to bless His people specifically so that His way and salvation would become known to every nation. It closes by connecting a good, tangible harvest to this same missionary hope.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm teaches that God's blessings on His covenant people were never meant to terminate on them alone; they exist so that the whole earth would come to know and praise the true God.
basic.originalLanguages: The prayer that God's panim, face, would shine on His people borrows the language of the Aaronic blessing, Numbers 6:24-26, but redirects it toward the missionary hope that all the earth would yare, fear, reverence, the true God.
basic.typology: This psalm's hope that 'all the ends of the earth' would come to fear God anticipates the Great Commission, where the risen Christ sends His disciples to make disciples of all nations, Matthew 28:19, fulfilling in the gospel what this psalm could only pray for in hope.
basic.christ: Christ is the fulfillment of this psalm's missionary hope, for in Him God's way and salvation have indeed become known among all nations, as the gospel goes out to every people at His own command, Matthew 28:19, and as every tribe, tongue, and nation is gathered to praise Him, Revelation 7:9-10.
basic.god: God is shown as gracious blesser of His own people, just judge and guide of every nation, and provider of the earth's tangible harvest, whose ultimate purpose in all of this blessing is that the whole earth would come to know and reverently fear Him.
basic.crossReferences: Numbers 6:24-26 supplies the priestly blessing this psalm echoes and redirects toward the nations. Matthew 28:19 records Christ's command to make disciples of all nations, the fulfillment of this psalm's missionary hope. Revelation 7:9-10 pictures a great multitude from every nation, tribe, and language praising God, the psalm's prayer answered in full.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's prayer that God's blessing on His people would make His way known among all nations finds its fulfillment in Christ, who sends His people to every nation with the gospel, gathering at last a multitude from every tribe and tongue to praise the God this psalm calls all peoples to worship.

context.literary: A brief, tightly structured harvest prayer framed by a repeated refrain, 'Let the peoples praise You, O God; let all the peoples praise You,' verses 3 and 5, moving from a request echoing the priestly blessing, verses 1-2, through universal praise, verses 3-5, to a closing report of harvest and hope, verses 6-7.
context.historical: Likely sung at a harvest festival in Israel's worship calendar, its prayer for a good harvest, verse 6, joined naturally to the older, missionary hope embedded in God's original call to Abraham, that through Israel all the families of the earth would be blessed, Genesis 12:3.
context.canonical: It stands among the Psalter's clearest missionary psalms, alongside Psalm 96 and Psalm 117, making explicit that Israel's blessing was always intended as a means toward the salvation and praise of every nation.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To ask God's blessing on His covenant people not as an end in itself, but explicitly so that His way and salvation would become known and praised among all the nations of the earth.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a specific prayer, likely at a harvest festival, for God's blessing to extend outward to the nations; it prescribes as an abiding pattern viewing every blessing believers receive as intended, at least in part, for the sake of making God known to others.

debates.question: Does the psalm's missionary hope, that 'all the ends of the earth' would fear God, reflect a genuinely universal vision within the Old Testament itself, or is it primarily a later Christian reading imposed on the text?

views.label: The psalm reflects a genuine, if not fully realized, universal vision already present within the Old Testament, rooted in God's original promise to Abraham that all nations would be blessed through Israel, Genesis 12:3.
views.case: The psalm's language, 'all nations,' 'the peoples,' and 'all the ends of the earth,' appears repeatedly and cannot be reduced merely to Israel's own experience; it explicitly reaches beyond Israel from within the psalm's own wording.

views.label: The psalm's primary concern is Israel's own blessing and harvest, with the hope for the nations functioning as a secondary, hoped-for byproduct rather than the psalm's central missionary purpose as later Christian readers have emphasized.
views.case: The psalm's concrete content, the priestly blessing and the harvest report, verses 1 and 6, centers on Israel's own experience, with the nations mentioned as recipients of the resulting testimony rather than as the psalm's primary subject.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the psalm explicitly names the nations as intended beneficiaries of Israel's blessing; whether read as the psalm's central purpose or a secondary hope, this missionary vision is genuinely present in the text and finds its full realization in the New Testament's mission to all nations.

study.originalLanguages: The prayer that God's panim, face, would shine on His people echoes the priestly blessing of Numbers 6:24-26, but this psalm redirects that blessing outward, so that all the earth would yare, fear, reverence, the true God, verse 7.

typology.type: The hope that God's way would be known on earth and His salvation among all nations, verse 2
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in the Great Commission, where the risen Christ sends His disciples to make disciples of all nations, Matthew 28:19.

typology.type: The closing hope that 'all the ends of the earth' would fear God, verse 7
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in the vision of a great multitude from every nation, tribe, and language gathered to praise God and the Lamb, Revelation 7:9-10.

study.christ: Christ is the fulfillment of this psalm's missionary hope, for in Him God's way and salvation have indeed become known among all nations, as the gospel goes out to every people at His own command, 'Go and make disciples of all nations,' Matthew 28:19. And the psalm's closing hope that 'all the ends of the earth' would fear God is answered in the vision of a great multitude from every nation, tribe, and language gathered to praise God and the Lamb forever, Revelation 7:9-10.
study.god: God is shown as gracious blesser of His own people, just judge and guide of every nation, and provider of the earth's tangible harvest, whose ultimate purpose in all of this blessing is that the whole earth would come to know and reverently fear Him.

crossReferences.note: Supplies the priestly blessing this psalm echoes and redirects toward the nations.
Records Christ's command to make disciples of all nations, the fulfillment of this psalm's missionary hope.
Pictures a great multitude from every nation, tribe, and language praising God, this psalm's prayer answered in full.

study.oneStory: This psalm's prayer that God's blessing on His people would make His way known among all nations finds its fulfillment in Christ, who sends His people to every nation with the gospel, gathering at last a multitude from every tribe and tongue to praise the God this psalm calls all peoples to worship.

quiz.q: What earlier biblical blessing does this psalm's opening verse echo?

options.text: The Aaronic priestly blessing of Numbers 6:24-26.
options.note: The prayer for God's grace and shining face directly echoes this earlier blessing, now redirected toward the nations.

options.text: The blessing given to Jacob before his death.
options.note: The psalm's specific language of grace and God's shining face echoes the priestly blessing of Numbers 6, not Jacob's deathbed blessing.

options.text: The blessing pronounced at Solomon's coronation.
options.note: No coronation blessing is echoed here; the language is from the priestly blessing of Numbers 6.

options.text: The blessing given to Noah after the flood.
options.note: The psalm's language specifically matches the Aaronic priestly blessing, not the blessing given to Noah.

quiz.q: According to verse 2, why does the psalmist want God to bless and be gracious to His people?

options.text: So that God's way and salvation would become known among all nations.
options.note: The blessing on Israel is explicitly requested for the sake of the nations knowing God.

options.text: So that Israel alone would prosper, with no concern for other nations.
options.note: The verse explicitly states the purpose extends to all nations knowing God's salvation.

options.text: So that Israel could defeat its enemies in battle.
options.note: The stated purpose is that God's way be known among the nations, not military victory.

options.text: So that the priests alone would have access to God's presence.
options.note: The purpose given extends to all nations, not merely priestly access.

quiz.q: What quality of God's rule over the nations does verse 4 highlight?

options.text: He judges the peoples justly and leads the nations of the earth.
options.note: This grounds the nations' invited joy in God's just and guiding rule over them.

options.text: He is indifferent to how the nations are governed.
options.note: The verse specifically credits God with just judgment and active leading of the nations.

options.text: He rules only over Israel, leaving other nations ungoverned.
options.note: The verse explicitly extends God's just judgment and guidance to the nations broadly.

options.text: He allows the nations to govern themselves entirely apart from Him.
options.note: The verse presents God as actively judging and leading the nations, not leaving them entirely to themselves.

quiz.q: What tangible sign of blessing does the psalm mention in verse 6?

options.text: The earth has yielded its harvest.
options.note: This concrete agricultural blessing grounds the psalm's larger missionary hope in a specific, tangible provision.

options.text: A military victory over a foreign army.
options.note: The tangible sign named is a harvest, not a military victory.

options.text: The construction of a new temple.
options.note: No temple construction is mentioned; the sign given is agricultural harvest.

options.text: The appointment of a new king.
options.note: The specific blessing named is the earth's harvest, not a political appointment.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament fulfill this psalm's closing hope that 'all the ends of the earth' would fear God?

options.text: In the vision of a great multitude from every nation, tribe, and language gathered to praise God and the Lamb.
options.note: Revelation 7:9-10 pictures exactly this psalm's hope realized in full.

options.text: In the complete destruction of every nation except Israel.
options.note: The fulfillment envisioned is worship gathered from every nation, not the destruction of all but Israel.

options.text: In a single future nation replacing all others entirely.
options.note: The fulfillment described is a multitude drawn from every existing nation, tribe, and language, not a single replacement nation.

options.text: In the abolition of all worship everywhere.
options.note: The fulfillment is the flourishing, not the abolition, of worship, now directed to God and the Lamb from every nation.

questions: The psalm prays for God's blessing on His people specifically so that His way would be known among the nations. What blessing has God given you that could become a means of making Him known to someone else?

The psalm's refrain calls 'all the peoples' to praise God, not Israel alone. Who in your life, from a different background or culture than your own, could you specifically pray would come to know and praise God?
Verse 4 grounds the nations' joy in God's just judgment and guidance over them. How does trusting that God actively governs even the nations, not just individuals, shape how you view world events?

The psalm connects a tangible harvest, verse 6, to its larger missionary hope. What ordinary, practical blessing in your own life this season could you specifically dedicate to God's larger purposes rather than keeping only for yourself?

This psalm's hope that all the earth would fear God is fulfilled in the Great Commission and in the vision of every nation gathered in worship. What is one concrete step you could take this week toward participating in that mission, whether through prayer, giving, or going?

translationNotes.note: Cause His face to shine upon us echoes the Aaronic priestly blessing of Numbers 6:24-26 nearly word for word, here redirected from a purely domestic blessing toward a missionary purpose.
Judge the peoples justly can also carry the sense of governing or guiding with equity, describing God's active, righteous rule over every nation, not only Israel.
Fear renders yare, reverent awe rather than mere terror, describing the right response of worship the psalm hopes will extend to every nation on earth.

meta.notes: BSB text retrieved via bible.com (YouVersion, Berean Standard Bible, version 3034) after the local makor-scripture toolkit failed with a persistent disk I/O error reading the sandbox-mounted database. Verse numbering and count (7 verses) verified against Psalm 67:1-67:7 per PSALMS-SECTION-MAP.md. The musical superscription is not rendered as verse text, consistent with the established convention in this corpus. No em or en dashes present. No sermon link could be verified this session from the trusted cluster for Psalm 67 specifically, so sermon status is none.
